sourceTitle: A documents database tailored for product teams

questions.question: Where do you go to add the Docs template to your Notion workspace?

options.text: Click the **Templates** option in the sidebar, search for **Docs**, and select **Get template**.
options.explanation: The article explains that you add the template via the Templates section in the sidebar, then search for Docs and click Get template.

options.text: Open any page, type **/docs**, and press Enter.
options.explanation: Typing /docs creates a block, not the full database template.

options.text: Navigate to **Settings → Integrations → Docs Template**.
options.explanation: There is no Settings‑Integrations path for adding the Docs template.

options.text: Upload a CSV file named **Docs.csv** to a new database.
options.explanation: Uploading a CSV creates a table of rows, not the pre‑built Docs template.

questions.question: Which property type would you use to show how many incomplete tasks are linked to a document?

options.text: Relation
options.explanation: A Relation links to another database but does not count items; you need a Rollup for counting.

options.text: Rollup
options.explanation: A Rollup can aggregate values from a related database, such as counting incomplete tasks.

options.text: Select
options.explanation: Select lets you choose a single value; it cannot compute counts.

options.text: Formula
options.explanation: While a Formula can calculate, you first need a Rollup to bring in the task data before a formula could be applied.

questions.question: If you want a view that only shows documents whose status is "In Review", which combination of actions is correct?

options.text: Create a new view, name it “To Review”, add a filter → Status = “In Review”.
options.explanation: The article describes exactly this workflow for a review‑focused view.

options.text: Duplicate the default table view and rename it “In Review”.
options.explanation: Renaming alone does not filter the items; you still need to add a filter.

options.text: Switch the existing board view to group by "In Review".
options.explanation: Board views group by a property, not filter by a specific value.

options.text: Add a new column property called “In Review” and sort by it.
options.explanation: Adding a column does not automatically filter; you’d still need a filter condition.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about database templates is FALSE?

options.text: Templates let you pre‑fill properties and sections so you only need to fill in the blanks for each new page.
options.explanation: This describes the core purpose of database templates correctly.

options.text: You can set a template as the default for every new entry in the database.
options.explanation: The article notes you can choose a default template for new items.

options.text: Templates can only be used for RFCs and PRDs, not for any other document type.
options.explanation: The guide explicitly says you can create any templates you want, not just RFCs or PRDs.

options.text: You edit a template by clicking the three‑dot menu next to its name and modifying the page that opens.
options.explanation: This matches the described method for editing templates.

questions.question: What advantage does adding a **Relation** property between the docs database and a tasks database provide?

options.text: It automatically creates a duplicate copy of every task inside each document.
options.explanation: Relations link records; they do not duplicate content.

options.text: It enables you to link a document to specific tasks, allowing you to see related work in one place.
options.explanation: Relations are meant for linking records across databases, which is exactly what the article suggests.

options.text: It changes the document’s status property to match the task’s status.
options.explanation: Relations do not sync property values; they only create a reference.

options.text: It converts the document into a Kanban board automatically.
options.explanation: Board views are a separate view type; a Relation does not affect layout.

questions.question: Which block type would you use to make a short, highlighted note that stands out from regular text?

options.text: Callout
options.explanation: The guide lists Callouts as the way to highlight a piece of text.

options.text: Divider
options.explanation: Dividers create a visual line, not a highlighted note.

options.text: Toggle list
options.explanation: Toggle lists hide content behind a clickable heading, not highlight it.

options.text: Quote
options.explanation: Quotes format text as a quotation, not as a highlighted callout.

questions.question: When would a **Calendar** view be most useful for a docs database?

options.text: When you want to see documents ordered by their creation date.
options.explanation: Creation date is not a date property that can be displayed on a calendar; calendar view relies on a specific date field.

options.text: When each document has a due‑date property and you need to visualize upcoming deadlines.
options.explanation: The article explains adding a due‑date property and then a calendar view to organize docs by that date.

options.text: When you need to group documents by their status (To Do, In Progress, Complete).
options.explanation: Grouping by status is done with a board view, not a calendar.

options.text: When you want to display the number of incomplete tasks per document.
options.explanation: That information is shown via a Rollup, not a calendar view.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the **"Mine"** saved view in the template?

options.text: To show every document that has the word “mine” in its title.
options.explanation: The view filters by the creator/editor, not by the word in the title.

options.text: To display documents you created or recently edited.
options.explanation: The article describes the “Mine” view as showing any pages you’ve created or recently edited.

options.text: To list all documents that are marked with the tag "Mine".
options.explanation: There is no tag called "Mine"; the view uses ownership, not tags.

options.text: To sort documents alphabetically by their title.
options.explanation: Sorting alphabetically is a sort setting, not the purpose of the "Mine" view.

questions.question: Which combination of steps would let you automatically generate a new RFC document every Monday?

options.text: Create an RFC template, set it as the default template, and enable the "Repeat" option with a weekly cadence on Monday.
options.explanation: The guide mentions you can schedule repeating templates, which allows automatic creation on a set cadence.

options.text: Add a Formula property that checks the current day and duplicates the template when it's Monday.
options.explanation: Formulas cannot create new pages; they only calculate values.

options.text: Create a board view grouped by "Day of Week" and manually drag the RFC template into the Monday column each week.
options.explanation: Board views are for organization, not automation of page creation.

options.text: Set a Reminder on the RFC template page for every Monday.
options.explanation: Reminders alert you; they do not generate new database entries.

questions.question: Why might a product manager choose to embed a Jira issue inside a PRD page?

options.text: To automatically sync the PRD’s status with the Jira issue’s status without any manual steps.
options.explanation: Embedding shows the issue, but does not create a two‑way sync of status.

options.text: To give readers quick visibility into related development work without leaving Notion.
options.explanation: The article highlights embedding content from tools like Jira to provide full visibility within Notion.

options.text: To replace the need for a separate tasks database in Notion.
options.explanation: Embedding supplements, not replaces, the tasks database; you still may need a tasks DB for tracking.

options.text: To convert the Jira issue into a Notion page automatically.
options.explanation: Embedding displays the issue; it does not convert it into a native Notion page.
